name: Ahura Mazda
title: Persian - Ahura Mazda

primarySources.title: The Gathas of Zarathushtra
primarySources.author: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster)
primarySources.text: The Gathas are a collection of hymns believed to be composed by Zoroaster himself. They are the oldest texts of the Avesta and provide the most direct insight into Zoroaster's teachings about Ahura Mazda. They emphasize Ahura Mazda's wisdom, justice, and role as the creator of the universe. They are the foundation of Zoroastrian theology, offering a glimpse into the relationship between Ahura Mazda and humanity, as well as the cosmic struggle between good and evil. They are considered the most sacred texts in Zoroastrianism.

primarySources.title: Yasna
primarySources.author: Various Zoroastrian Priests
primarySources.text: The Yasna is the primary liturgical collection of the Avesta. It includes the Gathas but also contains other hymns, prayers, and rituals used in Zoroastrian worship. The Yasna ceremony is a central act of Zoroastrian devotion, involving the recitation of Avestan texts and offerings to Ahura Mazda and other divine beings. It also includes details on the creation and the final renovation of the world, Frashokereti. The Yasna provides a more comprehensive view of Zoroastrian religious practice and beliefs than the Gathas alone.

primarySources.title: Bundahishn
primarySources.author: Unknown (compiled in the 9th century CE)
primarySources.text: The Bundahishn is a Zoroastrian text written in Middle Persian (Pahlavi). It provides a detailed account of Zoroastrian cosmology, mythology, and eschatology. It covers the creation of the world, the origin of humans, the struggle between Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu, and the eventual triumph of good over evil. The Bundahishn is a valuable source for understanding the later developments of Zoroastrian theology and mythology, as well as the cultural context in which these beliefs were transmitted. It gives detailed descriptions of the creation of the world by Ahura Mazda and the subsequent attack by Angra Mainyu, and how the world will eventually be purified and restored to its original perfection.

primarySources.title: Denkard
primarySources.author: Various Zoroastrian Scribes
primarySources.text: The Denkard is an encyclopedic work of Zoroastrian religious and philosophical thought, compiled in Middle Persian. It covers a wide range of topics, including theology, ethics, law, and cosmology. The Denkard contains summaries and interpretations of earlier Avestan texts, as well as original material. It is an important source for understanding the intellectual and religious history of Zoroastrianism, providing insights into the ways in which Zoroastrian beliefs were interpreted and applied in different historical contexts. It also includes discussions of the nature of Ahura Mazda and his relationship to the world.

primarySources.title: Visperad
primarySources.author: Various Zoroastrian Priests
primarySources.text: The Visperad is a collection of liturgical texts closely related to the Yasna. It consists of additional hymns and prayers that are recited during specific ceremonies and festivals. The Visperad focuses on the praise and veneration of Ahura Mazda and the other Amesha Spentas (divine beings). It provides further details on Zoroastrian rituals and beliefs, as well as insights into the relationship between the divine and the human realms. It is typically recited in conjunction with the Yasna, enhancing the solemnity and spiritual significance of the ceremonies.

primarySources.title: Zatspram: Anthology
primarySources.author: Zatspram
primarySources.text: A 9th-century Zoroastrian high priest that provides a narrative of Zoroaster's life, and insights into Zoroastrian beliefs and practices. It includes descriptions of the creation and the struggle between good and evil, and also Zoroastrian law.

primarySources.title: Boyce, Mary. Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices.
primarySources.author: Mary Boyce
primarySources.text: A comprehensive overview of Zoroastrianism, covering its history, theology, rituals, and social organization. Boyce is a leading scholar in Zoroastrian studies.

primarySources.title: Dhalla, Maneckji Nusservanji. Zoroastrian Theology: From the Earliest Times to the Present Day.
primarySources.author: Maneckji Nusservanji Dhalla
primarySources.text: A detailed examination of Zoroastrian theological concepts, including the nature of Ahura Mazda, the Amesha Spentas, and the struggle between good and evil.

deities.name: Apam Napat
deities._unverifiedReason: Target entity not found in database

description: Ahura Mazda, whose name translates to "Wise Lord" or "Lord of Wisdom," is the supreme deity in Zoroastrianism, an ancient monotheistic (or, according to some interpretations, dualistic monotheistic) religion originating in ancient Persia (modern-day Iran). He is considered the uncreated creator of all things, the source of all goodness, light, and order in the universe. As the embodiment of truth and righteousness, Ahura Mazda stands in stark contrast to Angra Mainyu (also known as Ahriman), the principle of evil and darkness. The worship of Ahura Mazda forms the cornerstone of Zoroastrian faith and ethical principles, emphasizing the importance of moral choices and the ultimate triumph of good over evil. He is also referred to as Ormazd or Ohrmazd in Middle Persian texts.  The origins of Ahura Mazda are deeply intertwined with the teachings of Zoroaster, also known as Zarathushtra, the prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism. Zoroaster's revelations, as recorded in the Gathas – a collection of hymns within the Avesta, the sacred texts of Zoroastrianism – present Ahura Mazda as the sole, transcendent God, distinct from the polytheistic deities prevalent in ancient Iran before Zoroaster's reforms. Zoroaster's revolutionary message emphasized the importance of *asha* (truth, righteousness, or cosmic order) and the individual's responsibility to choose between good and evil. Ahura Mazda represents the path of righteousness, guiding humanity towards moral perfection through *Spenta Mainyu*, his bounteous spirit, and the Amesha Spentas, divine beings who embody different aspects of his divine nature. The Gathas are considered the most direct and authoritative source for understanding Ahura Mazda's nature and his relationship with humanity. They portray a God who is not only powerful and wise but also deeply concerned with the moral choices of humankind.  Zoroastrian cosmology, as detailed in texts like the Bundahishn, describes Ahura Mazda's eternal existence in a realm of pure light and goodness. Before the creation of the material world, Ahura Mazda foresaw the existence of Angra Mainyu, the embodiment of evil and darkness. Knowing that Angra Mainyu would inevitably attempt to corrupt his creation, Ahura Mazda conceived the universe as a cosmic battleground for the ongoing struggle between good and evil. However, this struggle is not symmetrical. Ahura Mazda is the ultimate source of creation and all that is good, while Angra Mainyu is a destructive force, perpetually striving to undo the good that Ahura Mazda brings into existence. The Bundahishn vividly describes Ahura Mazda's creation of the spiritual world (Menog) and subsequently the material world (Getig). The initial creation was envisioned as perfect and without flaw, but Angra Mainyu immediately launched an attack, introducing evil, suffering, and death into the world. Despite Angra Mainyu's destructive actions, Ahura Mazda maintains ultimate control over the destiny of the universe and the eventual triumph of good.  The Amesha Spentas, often described as divine beings or archangels, are instrumental in assisting Ahura Mazda in governing the world and fulfilling his divine will. These beings represent different aspects of Ahura Mazda's divine nature and serve as intermediaries between the supreme God and humanity. They include: *Vohu Manah* (Good Mind), representing wisdom and good thoughts; *Asha Vahishta* (Best Truth), embodying righteousness and cosmic order; *Khshathra Vairya* (Desirable Dominion), representing righteous power and just rule; *Spenta Armaiti* (Holy Devotion), symbolizing piety, serenity, and devotion; *Haurvatat* (Wholeness), representing perfection and health; and *Ameretat* (Immortality), embodying eternal life and everlastingness. Together, Ahura Mazda and the Amesha Spentas work harmoniously to uphold cosmic order and promote the ultimate victory of good over evil. The Amesha Spentas are not merely abstract concepts; they are also seen as entities that can be invoked and venerated in Zoroastrian prayers and rituals.  The concept of *asha* (Truth, Righteousness, or Cosmic Order) is a central pillar of Zoroastrianism and is inextricably linked to Ahura Mazda. Asha represents the divine law that governs the universe and the moral principles that humans should adhere to. By living in accordance with *asha*, individuals contribute to the defeat of evil and the establishment of a world characterized by peace, justice, and harmony. This concept extends beyond mere adherence to rules; it encompasses a holistic approach to life that emphasizes ethical conduct, honesty, and compassion.  Angra Mainyu, the adversary of Ahura Mazda, embodies all that is evil, false, and chaotic in the universe. He constantly endeavors to undermine Ahura Mazda's creation and lead humanity astray. While Angra Mainyu is a formidable force, he is not considered an equal to Ahura Mazda. He is ultimately a destructive principle destined for defeat. The Zoroastrian worldview underscores the importance of human free will in the cosmic struggle between good and evil. Humans are not mere puppets of fate; they possess the ability to choose between the path of righteousness (following Ahura Mazda) and the path of wickedness (following Angra Mainyu). Through their thoughts, words, and deeds, individuals can either strengthen the forces of good or contribute to the spread of evil. The choices that humans make have profound cosmic consequences, influencing the balance of good and evil in the universe.  Zoroastrian eschatology includes the belief in a final renovation of the world known as *Frashokereti*, a transformative event in which evil will be completely eradicated, and the world will be restored to its original state of perfection. This event will be preceded by a final battle between good and evil, in which Ahura Mazda and his forces will triumph decisively over Angra Mainyu and his demonic legions. The righteous will be resurrected and rewarded with eternal life, while the wicked will face punishment for their transgressions. After the *Frashokereti*, the world will become a paradise of peace, justice, and immortality, where suffering and death will be no more.  The symbolism associated with Ahura Mazda is rich and multifaceted, reflecting the depth and complexity of Zoroastrian theology. The *Faravahar*, a winged disk with a male figure, is a prominent symbol of Zoroastrianism and is often interpreted as representing Ahura Mazda himself or the human soul striving towards spiritual enlightenment. The wings symbolize the capacity for spiritual ascent, while the tail feathers represent the three fundamental tenets of Zoroastrianism: good thoughts, good words, and good deeds. The male figure embodies the human soul's aspiration towards spiritual perfection and union with Ahura Mazda. Fire holds immense significance in Zoroastrianism, representing Ahura Mazda's divine light, purity, and creative energy. Zoroastrian temples, known as fire temples, house a sacred fire that is meticulously maintained and kept burning continuously as a tangible symbol of Ahura Mazda's eternal presence. The sun is also closely associated with Ahura Mazda, serving as a potent symbol of light, life, and divine illumination. The bull, often depicted in Zoroastrian art, symbolizes creation, fertility, and the life-giving power of Ahura Mazda. The white horse is associated with Ahura Mazda's purity, strength, and regal authority. The cypress tree, with its enduring evergreen foliage, represents immortality and eternal life. The *Haoma* plant, used in Zoroastrian rituals, is associated with healing, spiritual insight, and the attainment of divine knowledge. The pomegranate, a fruit brimming with seeds, symbolizes fertility, abundance, and the promise of new life.  Ahura Mazda's cultural significance extends far beyond the confines of the religious realm. Zoroastrianism has exerted a profound influence on Iranian culture, art, and literature throughout history. The ethical principles of Zoroastrianism, such as honesty, justice, compassion, and the pursuit of knowledge, have deeply shaped Iranian values and social norms. Zoroastrianism has also influenced other religions, including Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Some scholars posit that Zoroastrian concepts such as monotheism, the existence of angels and demons, the belief in a final judgment, and the concept of heaven and hell may have influenced the development of these Abrahamic faiths. Zoroastrianism served as the state religion of the Achaemenid, Parthian, and Sasanian empires of ancient Iran, during which Zoroastrian priests wielded considerable political and social influence.  The decline of Zoroastrianism commenced with the Islamic conquest of Persia in the 7th century CE. Today, Zoroastrian communities persist in Iran, India (where they are known as Parsis), and other parts of the world. Despite their relatively small numbers, Zoroastrians remain committed to preserving their ancient traditions, beliefs, and rituals. The concept of Ahura Mazda remains central to their faith, representing the eternal source of goodness, truth, and order in the universe. The ongoing struggle between Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu serves as a constant reminder of the importance of choosing good over evil and striving towards spiritual perfection in all aspects of life. Zoroastrianism emphasizes individual responsibility, ethical conduct, and the unwavering belief in the ultimate triumph of good over evil.  The Avesta, particularly the Gathas, provides the most direct and authoritative source for understanding Ahura Mazda's nature and role in the Zoroastrian worldview. Later Zoroastrian texts, such as the Bundahishn and the Denkard, offer further elaborations and interpretations of these core beliefs. These texts, in conjunction with the rituals and practices of Zoroastrianism, provide a comprehensive understanding of the faith's rich theological and cultural heritage. Ahura Mazda, as the Wise Lord, continues to inspire Zoroastrians to live lives of righteousness, compassion, and devotion to the pursuit of truth and goodness. He embodies all that is virtuous and serves as the ultimate source of hope for a world free from evil and suffering. The enduring legacy of Ahura Mazda stands as a testament to the enduring power of monotheistic faith and the timeless struggle between good and evil. The concept of Ahura Mazda also provides a lens through which to view the importance of free will and the power of individual choices in shaping the world around us. His enduring presence in Zoroastrianism highlights the importance of ethical considerations and the pursuit of knowledge as fundamental aspects of a meaningful life.

historicalEncounter.description: Zoroastrianism's monotheistic and dualistic elements made it a significant point of comparison for Jewish and Christian thought. Scholars have debated Persian influence on post-exilic Judaism (angelology, eschatology) and early Christianity.
historicalEncounter.significance: While avoiding simple borrowing theories, scholars recognize Zoroastrian contributions to religious vocabulary (heaven/hell imagery, angelology) that influenced Second Temple Judaism.
historicalEncounter.historicalNote: The Magi who visited Jesus (Matthew 2) were likely Zoroastrian priests, representing Persian religion's encounter with Christianity from its beginning.

typologicalConnections.observation: Ahura Mazda as supreme creator god represents Zoroastrian monotheism (or dualistic monotheism), the closest parallel to biblical monotheism in ancient religion.
typologicalConnections.distinction: Biblical monotheism is absolute; Zoroastrian dualism grants significant power to Angra Mainyu. God creates evil's possibility but is not opposed by an equal power.

typologicalConnections.observation: The Zoroastrian battle between Ahura Mazda (good) and Angra Mainyu (evil) parallels Christian spiritual warfare themes.
typologicalConnections.distinction: In Christianity, Satan is a fallen creature, not an eternal principle. Evil is parasitic on good, not co-eternal with it. Christ's victory is assured from the foundation of the world.

typologicalConnections.observation: Zoroastrian eschatology includes resurrection, final judgment, and cosmic renewal, remarkably paralleling Christian eschatology.
typologicalConnections.distinction: Whether Zoroastrianism influenced Judaism or both reflect common intuitions remains debated. Christian eschatology is Christ-centered; Zoroastrian eschatology is dualistic resolution.

magiAndChristianity.description: The Magi (Matthew 2:1-12) were probably Zoroastrian priests. Their recognition of Christ's birth suggests divine guidance of sincere seekers toward the truth.
magiAndChristianity.theologicalNote: The Magi narrative illustrates Acts 17:27 - God so arranged the world 'that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him.'

cyrusAndMessiah.description: Cyrus the Great, likely a Zoroastrian, is called God's 'messiah' (anointed one) in Isaiah 45:1, the only non-Israelite so designated. His decree freed the Jews from Babylonian exile.
cyrusAndMessiah.significance: This demonstrates God's sovereign use of foreign rulers and religions within his purposes.

scholarlyEnrichment.methodology: Comparative religion from Christian scholarly perspective, incorporating patristic sources and typological analysis

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda created the universe through his thought, first creating the spiritual world (Menog) and then the material world (Getig). This creation was initially perfect, but Angra Mainyu immediately attacked, introducing evil into the world, thus beginning the cosmic battle between good and evil.

keyMyths.title: The Amesha Spentas
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda is assisted by the Amesha Spentas, divine beings who embody different aspects of his divine nature. They represent virtues like Good Mind (Vohu Manah), Best Truth (Asha Vahishta), and Holy Devotion (Spenta Armaiti), aiding Ahura Mazda in governing the world and guiding humanity towards righteousness.

keyMyths.title: The Struggle Against Angra Mainyu
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda is in constant conflict with Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the embodiment of evil and darkness. This cosmic struggle represents the ongoing battle between good and evil, with Ahura Mazda ultimately destined to triumph and restore the world to its original perfection.

keyMyths.title: The Frashokereti (Final Renovation)
keyMyths.description: Zoroastrian eschatology culminates in the Frashokereti, a final renovation of the world where evil will be completely eradicated. This event will be preceded by a final battle between Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu, resulting in the resurrection of the righteous and the establishment of a world of peace and immortality.

keyMyths.title: The Choice of Free Will
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda granted humanity free will, allowing individuals to choose between good and evil. By choosing to follow the path of righteousness and living in accordance with *asha* (truth, righteousness), humans contribute to the defeat of evil and the triumph of Ahura Mazda.

extendedContent.title: Origins
extendedContent.content:
Ahura Mazda's origins are deeply intertwined with the teachings of Zoroaster (also known as Zarathustra), the prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism. Prior to Zoroaster's revelations, the religious landscape of ancient Persia consisted of a polytheistic system with various deities and spirits. Zoroaster, however, preached a radical new doctrine, proclaiming Ahura Mazda as the single, supreme, and uncreated God, the source of all goodness and light. This marked a significant shift towards monotheism, though some scholars interpret Zoroastrianism as dualistic due to the presence of Angra Mainyu, the destructive spirit and antithesis of Ahura Mazda.

Zoroaster's teachings, compiled in the Avesta, portray Ahura Mazda as the ultimate ethical authority, the embodiment of truth, justice, and wisdom. He is not merely a powerful being, but the very essence of goodness and the source of moral order in the universe. This ethical dimension is crucial to understanding Ahura Mazda's role, as adherence to his principles is seen as the path to salvation and the defeat of evil. The concept of Ahura Mazda represents a move away from capricious or arbitrary deities towards a God who is inherently benevolent and whose actions are guided by reason and righteousness.

The emergence of Ahura Mazda as the central figure in Zoroastrianism fundamentally redefined the relationship between humanity and the divine. Instead of appeasing multiple deities through sacrifices and rituals, Zoroastrians were called to actively participate in the cosmic struggle against evil by choosing good thoughts, good words, and good deeds. This ethical imperative, rooted in the worship of Ahura Mazda, laid the foundation for a unique and enduring religious tradition.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content:
The worship of Ahura Mazda in Zoroastrianism centers around maintaining purity and fighting against the forces of evil. Fire temples, known as Atash Behram, are central to Zoroastrian worship. Fire is considered a symbol of Ahura Mazda's light and wisdom, and it is kept perpetually burning, tended to by priests. Prayers are offered in the presence of the fire, and rituals are performed to maintain its sacredness. The Avesta, the sacred scriptures of Zoroastrianism, are recited during these ceremonies.

Daily prayers are an important aspect of Zoroastrian practice. Zoroastrians are encouraged to pray five times a day, facing a source of light, such as the sun, moon, or fire. These prayers involve reciting verses from the Avesta and reflecting on the teachings of Zoroaster. The prayers serve as a constant reminder of Ahura Mazda's presence and the importance of living a righteous life. Adherence to the principles of Asha, meaning truth and cosmic order, is paramount in their devotion.

Beyond formal rituals, the worship of Ahura Mazda extends to everyday life. Zoroastrians are encouraged to cultivate good thoughts, good words, and good deeds in all their actions. This ethical framework permeates all aspects of life, from personal conduct to social interactions. Acts of charity, honesty, and hard work are considered acts of worship, contributing to the overall struggle against evil and the advancement of Ahura Mazda's divine plan. This emphasis on ethical living distinguishes Zoroastrian worship from purely ritualistic practices, highlighting the importance of inner transformation and moral responsibility.

extendedContent.title: Iconography
extendedContent.content:
Unlike many other ancient religions, Ahura Mazda is not typically represented in a concrete, anthropomorphic form. Zoroastrianism generally discourages the use of idols or images in worship, emphasizing the abstract and transcendent nature of the divine. This aniconic tradition reflects the belief that Ahura Mazda is beyond human comprehension and cannot be adequately captured in physical form. Instead, symbolic representations are more common.

One of the most prevalent symbols associated with Ahura Mazda is the winged sun disk, known as the Faravahar. This symbol, though not exclusively representing Ahura Mazda (as it also symbolizes the human soul and divine glory), is frequently used in Zoroastrian art and architecture. The Faravahar typically depicts a winged disk with a male figure emerging from it, often holding a ring. The wings represent upward progress and spiritual ascension, while the ring symbolizes eternity and covenant. While the figure is sometimes interpreted as representing Ahura Mazda, it is more accurately seen as a representation of the human soul striving towards divine perfection.

While direct depictions of Ahura Mazda are rare, the concept of light is strongly associated with him. Fire, as mentioned previously, is a central symbol in Zoroastrianism and is seen as a manifestation of Ahura Mazda's divine light and wisdom. Therefore, depictions of fire altars and perpetually burning flames can be considered indirect representations of Ahura Mazda's presence. The absence of a definitive iconographic form underscores the emphasis on the abstract qualities of Ahura Mazda – his wisdom, goodness, and the incorporeal nature of the divine.
